extra.message: User-controlled input 'name' (from request.post() in views.py:57) is interpolated directly into SQL via Python '%' string formatting in Student.create(). The query 'INSERT INTO students (name) VALUES ('%(name)s')' % {'name': name} passes user input as part of the SQL string rather than as a parameterized argument, allowing SQL injection. All other DAO methods in this codebase use proper parameterized queries (e.g., cur.execute(q, params)), confirming this is an outlier vulnerability.

Jinja2 auto-escaping is globally disabled (autoescape=False). Multiple templates render user-controlled data without the |e escape filter, enabling stored XSS. Affected templates and unescaped variables: base.jinja2:25,39 (auth_user first/middle/last name), courses.jinja2:17-18 (course.title, course.description), course.jinja2:14-15,22 (course.title, course.description, review.review_text), students.jinja2:16 (student name), review.jinja2:32 (course.title). User can inject HTML/JavaScript via course titles, descriptions, review texts, and student names.

CSRF protection middleware is commented out in the Application constructor. The csrf_middleware (defined in sqli/middlewares.py:26-38) validates CSRF tokens on POST requests, but it is not included in the middleware chain. Templates include CSRF tokens via hidden <input> fields, but server-side validation never occurs, leaving all state-changing POST endpoints vulnerable to CSRF attacks.

The POST /students/ endpoint (students handler, lines 52-59) has no @authorize() decorator or any authentication check. An unauthenticated attacker can send a direct POST request to create students. The template hides the form behind {% if auth_user %}, but this is a client-side restriction only.

The POST /courses/ endpoint (courses handler, lines 84-90) has no @authorize() decorator or any authentication check. An unauthenticated attacker can send a direct POST request to create courses. The template restricts the form to admin users only (auth_user.is_admin), but the server-side view enforces no such restriction.

The POST /students/{student_id}/evaluate/{course_id} endpoint (evaluate handler, lines 135-153) has no @authorize() decorator. An unauthenticated attacker can submit evaluations and assign marks to any student in any course by sending a direct POST request. The template restricts the evaluation form to admin users only (auth_user.is_admin in course.jinja2:41), but the view has no server-side authorization check.

The POST /courses/{course_id}/review endpoint (review handler, lines 119-129) has no @authorize() decorator. An unauthenticated attacker can submit course reviews by sending a direct POST request. No server-side authentication or authorization check is performed.

User.check_password() uses MD5 to hash and compare passwords: md5(password.encode('utf-8')).hexdigest(). MD5 is cryptographically broken and unsuitable for password hashing. It is vulnerable to rainbow table attacks, collision attacks, and brute-force due to its speed. A secure key derivation function like bcrypt, scrypt, or Argon2 should be used instead.

cwe: CWE-327: Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm

extra.message: Session cookies are created with httponly=False, meaning the session cookie is accessible to client-side JavaScript. Combined with the disabled autoescaping (XSS vulnerability in app.py:35), this allows an attacker to steal session cookies via XSS and hijack user sessions.

The default application configuration file config/dev.yaml contains hardcoded database credentials (user: 'postgres', password: 'postgres'). This file is loaded as the default config in app.py:18 and committed to the repository.
